overview.body: Work through each Gaddis program in order. Type every program by hand rather than copying and pasting — the repetition builds muscle memory for dictionary syntax.
objectives: Read and run Program 9-1 to see dictionary creation syntax
Modify Program 9-2 to practice key access and iteration
Use Program 9-3 to understand the .get() method
Apply Program 9-4 patterns to sets and sorted output
overview.keyConceptsText: Chapter 9 covers dictionary creation, key access, iteration with .items(), the .get() method, and sets with sorted() for consistent output.
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis — Chapter 9
programRequirements: All four programs run without errors
Each program is in its own clearly commented section
overview.filenameNote: Submit bookex-ch09.py with all programs combined into one file, each in its own commented section.

content.body: Program 9-1 introduces dictionary syntax — curly braces, key-value pairs, and basic key access.

items: Open your textbook to Program 9-1 on the designated page.
Type the program exactly as written — do not copy/paste.
Run it and confirm the output matches the book.

content.title: Type It, Don't Copy It
content.body: Typing the program yourself is the point. Your fingers learn the syntax faster than your eyes do.
Program 9-2 shows how to loop over a dictionary using the .items() method to access both keys and values.

items: Run it and confirm output matches.
Try adding two more key-value pairs to the dictionary and run again.

content.title: Dictionary Order
content.body: As of Python 3.7, dictionaries maintain insertion order. Your output will match the order you added the keys.
Program 9-3 introduces .get(), which returns a default value when a key doesn't exist instead of raising a KeyError.

items: Run it once with a key that exists and once with a key that does not.

content.title: Direct Access vs .get()
content.body: Using my_dict['missing_key'] raises a KeyError. Using my_dict.get('missing_key', 'default') returns the default safely.
Program 9-4 introduces sets — unordered collections of unique values. The sorted() function is used to produce consistent output for grading.

items: Run it and observe that duplicates are eliminated automatically.
Wrap the set in sorted() when printing to get consistent ordering.

content.title: Always Use sorted() for Grading
content.body: Sets are unordered. CodeGrade compares your output to expected output line by line. Always print sets using sorted() to guarantee consistent ordering.

content.output:
The set contains:
Apple
Banana
Cherry

beforeYouSubmit: All four programs run without errors
Each program is in its own section with a comment header
Sets are printed using sorted() for consistent output
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: This is a Chapter 9 BookEx exercise. You are implementing Gaddis programs exactly as written, with minor modifications as directed.

ifYouGetStuck: Re-read the program in the textbook before debugging — a typo is usually the culprit
Run the program after each addition rather than all at once
Submit to CodeGrade early — it shows exactly which tests pass or fail
Come to Zoom or office hours if you're still stuck after two attempts
needHelp.bookexResourceNote: Chapter 9 BookEx Reference Guide — step-by-step breakdown of every program in this chapter
